coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of glowing carbon dots from Carica papaya waste pulp and their application as a label-freechemo probe for chromium detection in water
coredata.dc:creator: D., Pooja
Singh, Lovepreet
Thakur, Anupma
Kumar, Praveen
coredata.dc:description: The work presents the facile green synthesis of blue luminescent C-dots (CDs) using papaya waste (Carica papaya) as a precursor. The as-synthesized CDS exhibit a quantum yield of 23.7% and are of size < 10 nm, while their polymerized architecture is confirmed by NMR spectroscopic analysis. The presence of various carbonyl and carboxyl surface groups confirmedthrough FT-IR is utilized for their surface modification with Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) to detect chromium in water with high sensitivity and selectivity. The sensor demonstrated a fashioned decrease in fluorescence intensity on chromium (Cr (III), Cr (VI), Cr (III) + Cr (VI)) addition in a wide detection window of 10–1000 ppb. The theoretical detection (LOD, 3 s/sensitivity) and quantification (LOQ, 10 s/sensitivity) limits for total chromium were found to be 0.708 ppb and 2.4 ppb, respectively. The study presents fruit waste-derived and modified green CDS based chromium sensing platform for their potential integration with optical analyzers in water pollutants monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Papaya waste
Cr detection
Water analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs from Papaya waste
f-CDs for chromium sensing in water
Analytical performance of f-CDs towards total Cr

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed functionalization of green synthesized CDs with EDTA to obtain f-CDs.
Proposed mechanism of chromium sensing with f-CDs.
Optical characterizations of green synthesized CDs, (a) Absorption and emission characteristics, and (b) effect of excitation wavelength on PL emission characteristic.
(a) FT-IR (b) Raman characteristics of as-synthesized CDs, ligand EDTA and f-CDs.
Solid state13C–NMR (DMSO-d 6, 100 MHz, ppm) Spectrum of f-CDs.
HR-TEM and SAED of CDs (a & b) and f-CDs (c & d).
XPS Spectra of CDs and f-CDs, where (a) corresponds to survey scan, and (b), (c), (d) correspond to deconvoluted C(1 s), O(1 s), N(1 s) core-level spectra.
Sensing response of f-CDs towards various (a) cations and (b) anions.
Effect of (a) pH and (b) ionic strength on sensing response of f-CDs.

Sensing behavior of f-CDs towards chromium (a) Total Cr sensing studies with f-CDs and (b) Stern-Volmer plot of f-CDs towards total Cr for the linear range 10–100 ppb (inset: proposed model equation for total Cr detection).
Sensing response of f-CDs towards a mixture of multi-ions (anions and cations) at 0.1, 1 and 10 ppm concentrations.
Competitive ion study of f-CDs towards total chromium in presence of (a) cations, (b) anions and (c) multi-element mixture.
Carbon quantum dots based sensory probes for chromium detection.
Real water analysis for total chromium determination with f-CDs sensory probe.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of glowing carbon dots from Carica papaya waste pulp and their application as a label-freechemo probe for chromium detection in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh, 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad, 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata, 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work presents the facile green synthesis of blue luminescent C-dots (CDs) using papaya waste (Carica papaya) as a precursor. The as-synthesized CDS exhibit a quantum yield of 23.7% and are of size < 10 nm, while their polymerized architecture is confirmed by NMR spectroscopic analysis. The presence of various carbonyl and carboxyl surface groups confirmedthrough FT-IR is utilized for their surface modification with Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) to detect chromium in water with high sensitivity and selectivity. The sensor demonstrated a fashioned decrease in fluorescence intensity on chromium (Cr (III), Cr (VI), Cr (III) + Cr (VI)) addition in a wide detection window of 10–1000 ppb. The theoretical detection (LOD, 3 s/sensitivity) and quantification (LOQ, 10 s/sensitivity) limits for total chromium were found to be 0.708 ppb and 2.4 ppb, respectively. The study presents fruit waste-derived and modified green CDS based chromium sensing platform for their potential integration with optical analyzers in water pollutants monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium contamination of water resources is known to be originated from industrial discharge in surgencies such as electroplating, leather tanning, and dyestuff [1]. Though it is a required essential element for human health in regulating metabolic activities and maintaining blood sugar level, however, it is a well-documented genotoxin on consumption beyond a permissible limit of 100 ppb (total chromium) and 50 ppb (Cr (III) or Cr (VI)) in drinking water as restricted by WHO and BIS [2]. Many countries such as Germany, Japan, Egypt, Belgium, Bangladesh, and Brazil [3] are fronting its high level in natural waters, wherein Bangladesh is the worst affected amongst Asian countries [4]. While in India, several states including Gujarat, Haryana, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh and Punjab are under alarming position with respect to the presence of its high concentration (9–27,400 ppb) [5]. In water columns, Cr is found to mainly exist in Cr (III) and Cr (VI) valence states as oxyanions. The mechanism of chromium toxicity is explained in terms of its easy cellular engrossment through the anionic transport system induced by ascorbic acid, cysteine & glutathione reductant, binding capability with DNA and generation of free oxygen radicals. For instance, Cr(III) can extensively bind to the phosphate backbone of DNA, forming mutagenic Cr-DNA adducts, which during the cell transformation process are liable for altering the DNA code, causing cellular apoptotic death. The clinical symptoms of chromium toxicity are observed as hepatic & renal damages, gastrointestinal irritation, and CNS irritation [6]. Though Cr (VI) exhibits higher toxicity over Cr (III) due to its higher water solubility, oxidizing potential, and more rapid permeability through biological membranes [7], yet, it is equally important to monitor total chromium present in drinking water for mankind safety in wake of associated increasing health effects.

Therefore, several regulatory bodies employ conventional lab-basedtechniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, ion chromatography, ICP-MS, ICP-OES, for its detection, speciation, and monitoring in water resources [8]. But the expensive instrumentation, requirement of trained labor, complex sample processing and analysis time impedes their real deployment in the field. Alternatively, optical methods offer the advantage of simpler hardware and field portability, therefore, a variety of optical chemo probes are designed by researchers for chromium detection in water utilizing principles of colorimetry [9–13], absorbance [14,15], and photoluminescence (PL) [16–24], spectroscopy. However, the substantial benefit of PL spectroscopy in terms of higher sensitivity, simplicity and lower response time in sensor application can’t be neglected, yet a very limited research work is available on PL based detection of chromium (ESI, Fig. S1). In 2012, Aswathy et al. reported a highly selective colorimetric sensor for Cr (VI) utilizing Ag-NPs as a reductant for Cr (III) with subsequent aggregation of Ag-NPs, which appeared as the red-shift in Ag-NPs SPR peak [13]. Likewise, dithiocarbamate-modified N-benzyl-4-(pyridine-4-ylmethyl) aniline ligand (BP-DTC)modified gold nanoparticles (Au-NPs) are demonstrated for total Cr detection in aqueous solution to a good detection limit of 31 ppb. The probe is shown to detect Cr(III) as well as Cr(VI) in blood and wastewater samples, respectively [11].Later in 2013, Zhang et al. reported glutathione-stabilized gold nanoclusters (GSH-Au NCs) for selectivedetection of Cr(VI) to 0.5 ppb detection limits utilizing PL measurement approach. The as-synthesized fluorescent GSH-Au NCs employed the use of GSH as reducing reagent. The total chromium (Cr(III) and Cr(VI)) detection using GSH-Au NCs showed pH-dependent fluorescence quenching proficiencies, and thus exemplified selective determination of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) was achieved at different pH [25].

Of late CDs has gained a significant role as fluorescent sensor probe due to their easy synthesis, good photostability, biocompatibility, chemical robustness, and facile surface modifications. They are generally synthesized from chemical precursors utilizing sophisticated techniques such as laser ablation, elect thermal oxidation, arc-discharge, microwave/ultrasonic treatment, etc. [26]. Whilst to, green synthesis of CDs employs the use natural sources consisting of carbohydrate, lipids, macro/micro-nutrients, proteins, etc. and are successfully utilized in various applications [27]. Xiaoyu et al. were the first to use them as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for the Cr (VI) detection using “off-on” sensing mechanism due to inner filter effect (IFE). The probe was based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped CDs (PNCDs) [21]. In another work by Wanget al. highly luminescent N-doped CDs synthesized from citric acid and glycine (CG-CDs) are shown for label-free fluorescence-based detection of Cr(VI) in 5 to 200 μmol/L (R2 = 0.98) range with a detection limit of 4.16 μmol/L [22]. Though CDs based detection methodologies have greater advantages over other optical techniques, however,their synthesis using cost-effective chemicals, environmentally friendly solvents, and renewable materials is the key concern that should be taken into consideration. In this direction, Shelja et al., synthesized carbon nano-globules (CNG) from pineapple juice using hydrothermal synthesis method and investigated their Cr(VI) sensing applications [24]. More recently, Tyagi et al reported lemon juice derived CDs for Cr(VI) sensing in 2.5–50 μM concentration range [28]. However, to our knowledge best the green CDs derived from waste precursor are not investigated yet for total chromium analysis in water. Besides, the quantum yield of CDs in existing reports for chromium sensing is low, which is a determinant factor in deciding sensitivity of the sensor probe. Of late, there are two reports available in literature which state the use of papaya juice and powder as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CDs [29,30]. In contrast to, we here employ the use of papaya waste peels (Carica papaya pulp) as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs. Owing to papaya’s functional role, it’s a rich source of vitamin A and mineral elements such as calcium, magnesium, sodium and potassium [31]. The chemical composition of papaya juice constitutes of diverse acids including TN-butyric, n-hexanoic and n-octanoic acids, lipids; myristic, palmitic, stearic, linoleic, linolenic acids-vaccenic acid and oleic acids [32]. Whereas, the peels of papaya are a rich source of carbohydrates and fiber [33], which is utilized here as a carbon precursor.

In view of these facts, we herein report green synthesized CDs from papaya as a conversion strategy of waste to value-added product for total chromium (i.e. mixture of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in equal ratios) sensing in water. To achieve selectivity, the as obtained CDs are modified with Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a chelating ligand to achieve better sensitivity and selectivity over commonly observations in water. Chromium is known to exhibit notable chemical reactivity with nitrogen (Cr-N), oxygen (Cr-O), sulphur (Cr-S) and hydrogen(Cr-H) elements with the binding energies of 378, 427, 339 and 280 kJ/mol, respectively [34]. Earlier work reports use of several N, O rich ligands (ESI, Fig. S1) such as 1,6-bis (diphenyl hydroxyl methyl) pyridyl, diethylenetriaminepenta acetic acid (DTPA), dicaffeoltartaric acid(DCTA), 1,1,1-trifluoroacetylacetone(HTFA), cupferron, glutathione(GSH), ammonium pyrrolidine dithiocarbamate(APDC), etc., which can bind to chromium through selective coordination with oxygen, sulphur or nitrogen functional chelating groups [35,36]. In the present case, EDTA has been surface functionalized onto CDs surface using coordination chemistry to selectively bind chromium through its amines and carboxylates groups [37].

CDs were synthesized from the waste pulp of Carica papaya (known as papaya fruit), as a sole carbon precursor. EDTA obtained from LobaChemie was used for surface modification of as-synthesized CDs. Dialysis membrane (MWCO: 0.3 kDa, Spectrum Labs) was used for purification of both CDs and f-CDs before sensing experimentation. Cr (III) and Cr (VI) stock solutions (1000 ppm) procured from inorganic ventures were used to carry out all sensing experiments. Standard stock solutions(1000 ppm) of various ions (I−, PO4 2-, SO4 2-, Br−, Na+, Li2+,Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+,Zn2+,Pb2+,Cd2+,Co2+, Ni2+),multi-element mixtures such as M1 (SO4 2-, Cl-, Br-, F-), M2 (PO4 2-, NO3-,Cl-, SO4 2-), M3 (Mg2+, Ca2+, Li2+, Na+, Cu2+), etc., purchased from Merck & Inorganic Ventures were used for interference study. Thus obtained chemicalswere used as such without any further purification. Deionized water from Millipore corporation grade water system (Milli-Q) was used for all dilutions.

Optical characteristics of synthesized CDs and EDTA functionalized CDS (fCD) were probed by UV–vis (Hitachi U-3010) and fluorescence (Varian Cary Eclipse) spectrophotometer. The bright blue fluorescence of dots was seen and photographed under UV cabinet (POPULAR INDIA).The surface functionalization of CDs with EDTA was validated with FT-IR (Varian 670 IR), NMR (400 MHz, Avance-II spectrometer, Bruker Corporation) and XPS (Omicron)characterizations. The size of CDs was analyzed with HR-TEM(H-7500, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The pH measurements of all the solutions were made by a digital pH meter (LMPH 10, Labman Scientific Instruments Ltd).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs from Papaya waste
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by a facile pyrolysis route, while optimization of parameters in terms of synthesis time and the temperature was done to obtain highly luminescent CDs [38]. Briefly, precursor extract of the green waste pulp of Carica papaya pulp waste was extracted in boiled water, which was then subjected to pyrolysis at high temperature in a hot air oven. The observed pale yellow thick residue at the end of the process was dissolved in deionized water and stored at 4 °C until used. Thus obtained CDs showed bright blue luminescence under UV-trans illuminator. Further, their quantum yield (QY) was calculated using QS as reference dye (QY: 54% in 0.5 M H2SO4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: f-CDs for chromium sensing in water
ce:section.ce:para: To impart selectivity on CDS surface, they were modified with EDTA to selectively capture chromium in water. The surface modification was done by refluxing purified CDs with 50 mM EDTA at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain f-CDs, Scheme 1 , which were later, dialyzed against water to completely remove leftover free unreactive moieties and non-functionalized CDs. For sensing experiments, firstly the effect of pH was studied in wide pH window, and thereafter optimal conditions were used for investigating their sensing response towards Cr (III), Cr (VI) and total Cr in 10–1000 ppb concentration range. To ensure selectivity of the designed green probe, the comparative and competitive ion study against various interfering ions. For instance, competitive ion study was conducted with I−, PO4 2-, SO4 2-, Br−, Na+, Li2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+and multi-element mixtures such as M1 (SO4 2-, Cl-, Br-, F-), M2 (PO4 2-, NO3-,Cl-, SO4 2-), M3 (Mg2+, Ca2+, Li2+, Na+, Cu2+) at 100 ppb, 1000 and 10,000 ppb concentrations. While the interfering ions were taken at 1:1 with that of total Cr at 100 ppb concentrations for competitive ion study in the presence of total Cr. The real samples analysis was also done with water samples collected from different locations of Punjab.

CDs are known to exhibit unique optical properties due to the presence of aromatized carbon as their constituent unit. Fig. 1 (a) shows the absorption and luminescence characteristics of green synthesized CDs recorded in the range of 250–600 nm. The presence of absorption maxima peak at ∼340 nm is explained to be originated from n to π* and π to π* transitions with the carbon-carbon, carbon-oxygen molecular unsaturated centers [39], while the inset shows the blue coloured CDs under UV light, which indicates that the green synthesized CDs are fluorescent in nature. The PL optical spectrum recorded for these CDs shows characteristics emission at 462 nm. The origination of fluorescence in green CDs is hypothesized to the presence of sp2 cluster states within sp3 matrix, surface defects, particle size, emissive traps, the degree of oxidation, etc. It is observed from Fig. 1(b), the emission characteristics of synthesized CDs are nearly excitation-independent, with the maximum emission intensity at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The excitation-independent emission of the CDs denotes that both the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in CDsmust be uniform [40]. Further, the PL emission study of f-CDs also showed excitation independent emission behaviour, while EDTA didn’t show any fluorescence of its own (ESI, Fig. S2). The calculated QY of papaya pulp derived CDs from integrated intensities (excited at 310 nm), and the value of absorbance at 310 nm with reference dye (QS) was found to be ∼23.7% (ESI, Fig. S3), which is highest on green CDs demonstrated for Cr(VI) sensing. To get insight into the various surface functionalities onto the surface of CDs and f-CDs, the FT-IR spectral analysis was done and results are shown in Fig. 2 (a) (ESI, Table S1). The presence of vibrational peaks in CDs for CC, CO, −OH and −COOH associated with stretching mode of aromatic hydrocarbons, oxygenic groups, confirm their presence in CDs architecture, which also reveals their hydrophilic nature and openness towards surface modification with the appropriate ligand as per application. While the characteristic peaks at 1250, 1680 and 3440 cm−1 in EDTA spectra are attributed to C–N, CO and −OH stretch in their molecular structure. On EDTA functionalization to CDs, vibrational peak at 1644 cm−1 is observed and is understood in terms of EDTA functionalization on CDs, thus, forming carbonyl-amide bond. Furthermore, the presence of vibrational peaks at 1742 and 3443 cm−1 further ensure presence of −COOH, and −OH rich moieties onto f-CDs surface. Raman spectral analysis is an important tool to understand the molecular picture of the carbonaceous nanostructure, therefore both CDs and f-CDs were characterized by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2(b)). The presence of spectral D and G bands at ∼1372 cm−1 and ∼1638 cm−1, respectively, confirms their carbonaceous nature, while the comparatively high intensity of D over G bands in CDs can be attributed to the presence of confined sp3 defects within sp2 clusters. The positions remain same in case of f-CDs, which ensure no structural change in CDs upon functionalization as also confirmed by PL spectroscopic analysis of f-CDs.

Fig. 4 is associated with HR-TEM images of as-synthesized CDs and f-CDs, which clearly portrays their spherical morphology with an average diameter of ∼ 7 nm. The lattice fringes analysis of CDs reveals inter-particle spacing of ∼0.31 nm, which is on the higher side than normal graphene sheet. The deviation in d spacing represents the graphitic carbon nature of synthesized CDs, revealing their amorphous nature. The XRD analysis is in line with above observations, confirming their amorphous nature due to the presence of broad diffraction peak in between 20˚- 25˚, which is attributed to the disordered carbon atoms present in the structure (ESI, Fig. S4).

To understand the elemental composition and their surface states in CDs and f-CDs, XPS analysis of the structures was performed and the resultant data is shown in Fig. 5 , where (a) corresponds to survey scan, while (b), (c) and (d) belong to Gaussian deconvoluted C(1 s), O(1 s) and N(1 s) core level spectra into their components. The survey scan clearly reveals the presence of nitrogen for the f-CQDs. The C(1 s) core level for CQDs show the presence of CC, C–O and CO related components, whereas the additional peak at 285.7 eV for f-CQDs, meanest the presence of C–N bonds at the surface, which further confirmed by the N(1 s) spectra. The deconvolution of O(1 s), shows the increase in CO related contrition as compared to C–O for the f-CQDs [41]. Overall, XPS results well support the inferences drawn by FTIR and Raman analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of f-CDs towards total Cr
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to facile surface modification of CDs in lieu of various functional groups present on their surface, they can be easily modified with the desired ligand to impart selectivity. EDTA is a well-known metal chelator and is therefore used for removal of metal ions by researchers. In the present study, green synthesized CDs are surface modified with EDTA to impart nitrogen groups on their surface for selective binding of Cr utilizing coordination chemistry, as shown in Scheme 2 . Fig. 6 shows the sensing response of f-CDs towards various anions and cations commonly present in water. It can be seen very clearly that a decrease in PL intensity of f-CDs is observed on adding total Cr (i.e. Cr (III) and Cr (VI)), which is more prominent on the cumulative addition of both i.e. total chromium (ESI, Fig. S5). However, this is not the case with other ions, which confirm the selectivity of designed probe towards chromium. The observed decrease in fluorescence is explained in terms of non-irradiative recombination of charge carriers in f-CDs, upon selective binding of electron deficient Cr ions to nitrogen and oxygen groups present on the f-CDs surface [42]. Furthermore, elimination of surface vacancies and inner filter effect may also contribute to an observed decrease in intensity. In order to optimize the performance of sensor probe, several analytical parameters such as pH, ionic strength, etc., and the observed behaviour is shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen very clearly that the designed probe exhibit highest PL intensity in 5–7 pH range, which strengthens its application in a physiological environment. Before carrying out total chromium sensing studies, the complex is individually investigated against Cr (III) and Cr (VI), which reveals that the designed probe is capable to detect both Cr(III) and Cr (VI), however, the fitted model varies w.r.t. to change in PL intensity of the f-CDs and is almost linear (ESI, Fig. S6), yet no difference in sensing response time was observed. A higher decrease in intensity is observed for Cr (VI) over Cr (III). However, the present probe exhibits the response against both the oxidation states of Cr, it, therefore, offers the advantage of direct sensing of total chromium present in a water sample, without undergoing oxidation step to oxidize Cr(III) to Cr (VI), which makes this probe unique for field application. In comparison, as synthesized CDs didn’t show a significant sensing performance [ESI, Fig. S7], as observed in case of f-CDs, which could be attributed to availability of more selective chelating groups due to EDTA functionalization. Fascinated by the observed sensing response of designed probe, we have further studied its detection window over a wide concentration range of 10–1000 ppb. It can be seen clearly from Fig. 8 (a), that with increasing chromium concentration, a fashioned decrease in PL intensity of f-CDs is observed, which is also plotted as a ration of fluorescence intensity of f-CDs in the absence (Io) and presence (I) of chromium, shown in Fig. 8 (b). The developed sensory system showed a good linear relationship w.r.t. total Cr concentration at a level below WHO and BIS guidelines of 100 ppb. Besides, the observed analytical performance of f-CDs was also fitted with a best linear fit model in 10–100 ppb concentration range (Fig. 8(b), inset)) and found to be y = 190 ( 1 - x ), where y is total Cr concentration (ppb) and x =
F
F O
. The validation of these predicated concentration values from the proposed equations were also done with various experimental values (ESI, Table S2). The association constant of the f-CDs-Cr complex, calculated using Benesi-Hildebrand method is found to be 0.23 × 109 M−1 (ESI, Fig. S8). The observed high value of association constant is assigned to Cr-N and Cr-O coordination chemistry, which has also been adopted in previous reports with ligands such as diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), dicaffeoltartaric acid(DCTA), 1,1,1-trifluoroacetyl acetone(HTFA), ammonium pyrrolidinedithiocarbamate (APDC), owing to presence of N and O containing functional groups.

The theoretical detection (LOD, 3 s/sensitivity) and quantification (LOQ, 10 s/sensitivity) limit of f-CDs towards total Cr was determined to be 0.708 ppb and 2.4 ppb, respectively, where, s is the standard deviation of blank runs, which is 7.2 for ten replicates (n = 10) and sensitivity is 30. Further, for a triplicate set of experiments, relative standard deviation and precision of experiments (%) values were measured to be superiorthan1.26% and 0.01%, respectively. The experiential analytical characteristics manifest that the developed probe is highly reproducible, repeatable and sensitive towards total Cr detection in water. The Stern-Volmer (SV) quenching constant (Ksv) for quencher (S/N ration = 3) with fitting parameter (R2) of 0.9674 using SV plot (Fig. 8(b)) for linear regression equation Io/I= (5.49 × 108)C + 5.189 in 10–100 ppb linear concentration range was found to be 5.49 × 106 M−1. Further, the relative fluorescence quenching efficiency of chromium (% Ksv) at 10–100 ppb concentrations is calculated and shown in (ESI, Fig. S9) which supports the noticed quenching for different total Cr concentrations.

Furthermore, the selectivity performance of f-CDs sensory probe was evaluated against mixture of interferents/ions such as M1 (SO4 2−, Cl-, Br-, F-), M2 (PO4 2−, NO3-,Cl-, SO4 2−), M3 (Mg2+, Ca2+, Li2+, Na+, Cu2+) at 100 ppb, 1000 and 10,000 ppb concentrations. Fig. 9 shows the plot the change in fluorescence intensity as F/F0 in presence and absence of total Cr, which clearly manifest negligible interference of these commonly reported interferents/ions in water. The results clearly indicate that these interferents/ions don’t induce any structural and optical transformations of f-CDs. Moreover, to realize the practical application of the developed optical probe for total Cr detection, we have also performed the competitive ion study with various anions, cations, and multi-ions as shown in Fig. 10 . The response of probe against Cr(III) and Cr(VI), individually in the presence of above studied interfering ions is also investigated (ESI, Fig. S10). The interference study concludes the high selectivity of presented probe towards chromium even in the presence of other interfering metal ions, which could be understood in terms of strong Cr-N and Cr-O chemistry. The analytical performance of the developed probe is compared with existing work [21,22,24,28,43–48] shown in Table 1 which clearly depicts that the presented probe exhibit better sensitivity, detection limit, and window, which could be assigned to its high quantum yield over other similar reported green synthesized CDs based probes.

The probe was further tested for the real water samples collected from the ground, tap and well. All the real water samples were initially filtered to remove suspended particles using a 0.22 μm membrane followed by the spiking with total Cr solution to achieve 100 ppb concentration. The percentage recovery was found in 95–99% range (Table 2 ) and the results were in line with IC analysis of these samples.

In conclusion, we have synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots via one-step green synthesis approach using papaya waste as a precursor material. Thus, obtained CDs are surface modified with EDTA and demonstrated for total chromium detection in water with a theoretical detection limit of 0.708 ppb. The developed chemo-sensor could detect both Cr(III) and Cr(VI), without any pre-reduction/oxidation step, making it unique sensor system. The presented sensor probe exhibits the highest QY amongst various green synthesized CDs reported till today and is highly selective, sensitive, inexpensive, and label-free fluorescent chemosensory system for total chromium analysis in water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The constant support from the Prof. R. K. Sinha, Director, CSIR-CSIO is acknowledge PD acknowledges the DST, New Delhi for SYST project (GAP 375) and AT acknowledges DST-INSPIRE fellowship for her doctoral study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Pooja is working as a scientist in Agrionics division of Central Scientific Instruments Organisation. Her area of research work is in domain of material science, sensors, water analysis and energy harvesting. She is a recipient of several prestigious fellowships including BRICS young scientist fellow, commonwealth fellowship, ACS-PITTCON grant, GATE-MHRD fellowship, etc. She has published more than 40 research papers in reputed journal at very young age.
Lovepreet singh is a master student of Nano science and Nanotechnology. His research interest lies in nanomaterial synthesis through green chemistry route.
Anupma Thakur is a doctoral student in Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research. She is also a recipient of DST-INSPIRE fellowship. Her research focus is on sensors and energy harvesting.

Dr. Praveen is working as an Assistant Professor in Material Science department of Indian Association for Cultivation of Science, Kolkata. His research interest lies in III-V and carbonaceous nanostructures for solar cells, energy harvesting and sensors. He has published more than 56 research papers in international journals of repute.
